nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shorelines and sandy sublittoral zones of Lake Malawi; males patrol sandy patches adjacent to rocky outcrops where invertebrates are hunted by sifting the substrate

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep for sand-sifting behaviour
rocky caves and overhangs for territory and refuge
minimal or no rooted plants (will be uprooted); hardy Vallisneria or floating plants tolerated
stacked limestone or holey rock to raise pH buffering

diet.notes: In the wild, the Nyassae Peacock hunts invertebrates buried in sand, detecting prey by the faint water movements they produce — a specialised ability tied to the enlarged sensory pits visible along the lower jaw and cheek (part of the lateral line system). In the aquarium, it generally accepts high-quality cichlid pellets as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp. Feeder fish and mammalian-protein-based foods such as beef heart are best avoided, as these have been linked to Malawi bloat, a serious digestive condition. Vegetable matter should be kept minimal; spirulina-enriched foods are generally fine in small amounts.

goodWith: other Aulonocara species (peacock cichlids) of similar size
large Synodontis catfish (e.g., S. petricola) as dither fish
Labidochromis caeruleus (Yellow Lab) — relatively peaceful mbuna
avoidWith: aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Maylandia, Pseudotropheus) — harassment and hybridisation risk
other Aulonocara males without sufficient females and territory (male-on-male aggression)
small tetras or other small community fish (will be eaten or harassed)
other mouthbrooding Aulonocara congeners if hybrid prevention is desired

imageCandidates.notes: Klaus Rudloff, 1600x1200. Vibrant mature male, tack-sharp, whole fish in frame showing the diagnostic intense iridescent blue with yellow/white dorsal-fin margin. Small corner watermark only.
Cymothoa exigua, 975x641. Sharp iridescent-blue male photographed over fine sand substrate, illustrating the natural sand-sifting habitat.
Jan Sevcik, 750x500. Sharp red/orange spangled male representing a common trade colour line; adds colour variety. Modest corner watermark.

summary: The Nyassae Peacock (Aulonocara nyassae) is a brilliantly coloured cichlid from Lake Malawi, prized for the intense iridescent blues and yellows males develop at maturity. It is a sand-sifting insectivore that requires hard alkaline water and a fine sand substrate. The older name A. nyassae remains in wide use though some taxonomic treatments regard it as a synonym of A. stuartgranti.
careNotes: The Nyassae Peacock is an intermediate-level cichlid that rewards keepers who replicate its rift-lake home. Water chemistry is non-negotiable: maintain pH 7.8–8.6, GH 10–20 dGH, KH 10–18 dKH, and temperature 24–28 °C. Hard tap water often suits Lake Malawi species without additional treatment; in soft-water regions, buffer the tank with crushed coral, limestone rock, or a Malawi mineral blend. A fine sand substrate of at least 5 cm depth is essential — males sift mouthfuls of sand to locate buried invertebrates, a distinctive foraging mode linked to the enlarged sensory pits visible along the lower jaw and cheek. Without sand, this natural behaviour cannot be expressed. Aquascape with stacked rocks to provide caves and visual barriers that reduce aggression, while leaving open sandy clearings for foraging. A minimum 200 L tank with a 120 cm footprint suits a typical group; a larger tank is strongly preferred when mixing multiple peacock species. A ratio of one male to three or more females is generally advised to prevent prolonged harassment of any single female. Males tend to be territorial toward one another, particularly in smaller tanks. Mixing with aggressive mbuna is best avoided, as persistent harassment stresses peacocks and tends to suppress male colouration. For feeding, a high-protein carnivore cichlid pellet works well as a staple, supplemented regularly with frozen bloodworm, daphnia, and brine shrimp. High-fat mammalian proteins such as beef heart are best avoided, as these have been associated with Malawi bloat, a bacterial condition that can be difficult to treat. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are generally recommended to maintain water quality and keep nitrate levels in check. A. nyassae was the founding type species of the Aulonocara genus, described by Regan in 1922, but subsequent revisions treat it as either a synonym of A. stuartgranti or as a restricted-range species from the Nkhata Bay area of Lake Malawi. Fish sold in the trade as ‘A. nyassae’ or ‘Emperor Cichlid’ are often undifferentiated peacock lines or hybrids rather than pure A. nyassae.
breedingNotes: The Nyassae Peacock is a maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning is typically initiated by providing flat, open sandy areas in the tank; the male establishes a loose territory and courts females by flaring his fins and displaying his lateral colouration. After the female deposits eggs on the substrate, she immediately takes them into her mouth for incubation — a strategy that protects the eggs from predation. Clutch sizes typically range from 12 to 30 eggs, with incubation lasting approximately 21 to 28 days at 26 °C. After releasing the free-swimming fry, the female continues to shelter them in her mouth for the first several days. Moving the brooding female to a separate 80 to 100 L tank is generally recommended to protect her from tankmate harassment and to improve fry survival. Experienced breeders sometimes practice strip brooding — removing the eggs from the female's mouth at around day 14 to 16 and tumbling them artificially — which allows females to resume feeding and spawning more quickly. Fry accept freshly hatched brine shrimp and finely powdered cichlid fry food from their first day of free swimming. Hybridisation with co-housed Aulonocara relatives occurs readily, so keeping only one Aulonocara species per tank is advised where line purity matters.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aulonocara nyassae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~15 cm males), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, temperature 24–26 °C, hardness 179–447 ppm), native range (Lake Malawi), diet (invertebrates — sand-sifting), breeding mode (maternal mouthbrooder), and taxonomy notes on A. nyassae / A. stuartgranti synonymy debate.

additional.name: FishBase — Aulonocara nyassae
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 15.3 cm TL), depth range (3–25 m), temperature (24–27 °C), native range (endemic to Lake Malawi), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes). Confirms carnivore/invertivore diet and Lake Malawi endemism.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Peacock Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-level care guide for Aulonocara peacock cichlids. Confirmed tank size (minimum 55 gal / ~210 L), water parameters (pH 7.8–8.5, temp 76–82 °F / 24–28 °C), diet (protein-rich carnivore foods), and compatibility with other Malawi Haps/peacocks. Notes on Malawi bloat prevention and tank setup.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Aulonocara species
additional.notes: UK hobbyist/expert feature on peacock cichlids covering A. nyassae taxonomy history (original type species of the genus, Regan 1922), sand-sifting lateral-line sensory behaviour, group composition advice (1 male: 3+ females), and mbuna compatibility warnings. Supported taxonomy note and careNotes content.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum.com — Aulonocara nyassae species profile
additional.notes: Community expert species profile. Corroborated pH 7.8–8.6, minimum tank size 200 L, substrate requirements (fine sand), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 12–30 eggs, ~21–28 day incubation), and hybridisation risk with other Aulonocara species.

fish.breedingNotes: The Nyassae Peacock is a maternal mouthbrooder; the female incubates 12 to 30 eggs in her mouth for approximately 21 to 28 days. A separate brooding tank is recommended to protect the female from tankmate harassment and to improve fry survival. Hybridisation with other Aulonocara species occurs readily, so keeping only one Aulonocara species per setup is advised where line purity matters.
